content.problemStatement:
Chef has 333 hidden numbers A,B,A, B,A,B, and CCC such that 0≤A,B,C≤N0 \leq A, B, C \leq N0≤A,B,C≤N.

Let fff be a function such that f(i)=(A⊕i)+(B⊕i)+(C⊕i)f(i) = (A \oplus i) + (B \oplus i) + (C \oplus i)f(i)=(A⊕i)+(B⊕i)+(C⊕i). Here ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes the bitwise XOR operation.

Given the values of f(0),f(1),…,f(N)f(0), f(1), \dots, f(N)f(0),f(1),…,f(N), determine the values of A,B,A, B,A,B, and CCC.

It is guaranteed that **at least** one tuple exists for the given input. If there are multiple valid tuples of A,B,CA, B, CA,B,C, print any one.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN denoting the upper bound on the values of A,B,CA, B, CA,B,C.
Next line contains N+1N+1N+1 space-separated integers denoting f(0),f(1),…,f(N)f(0), f(1), \dots, f(N)f(0),f(1),…,f(N).




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, three space-separated integers, the values of A,B,A, B,A,B, and CCC. If there are multiple valid tuples of A,B,CA, B, CA,B,C, print any one.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^5). The second line contains N+1 space‑separated integers f(0), f(1), …, f(N).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output three space‑separated integers A, B and C (0 ≤ A, B, C ≤ N) on a single line. Any tuple that satisfies the given f values is accepted.

solution.approach:
For a fixed i we have A⊕i = A + i – 2·(A & i). Summing for the three numbers gives
    f(i) = (A+B+C) + 3·i – 2·[(A&i)+(B&i)+(C&i)].
Define S = A+B+C and for each bit k let cnt_k = a_k+b_k+c_k (0…3) where a_k,b_k,c_k are the k‑th bits of A,B,C. Then
    f(i) = S + Σ_k i_k·2^k·(3 – 2·cnt_k).
If we take i = 0 we get S = f(0). For i = 2^k (only the k‑th bit set) the formula reduces to
    f(2^k) – f(0) = 2^k·(3 – 2·cnt_k).
Hence cnt_k = (3 – (f(2^k)–f(0))/2^k) / 2, which is guaranteed to be an integer between 0 and 3.
After obtaining cnt_k for every bit up to the most significant bit of N we know the sum of bits at each position. To construct any concrete (A,B,C) we distribute the cnt_k ones greedily: put a 1 into A if cnt_k>0, decrease cnt_k; then into B, then into C. This always yields numbers ≤ N because we never set a bit that was not present in the original tuple.
The algorithm therefore:
1. Read the whole array f.
2. Compute S = f[0].
3. For each k with (1<<k) ≤ N compute delta = (f[1<<k] – S) / (1<<k) and cnt_k = (3 – delta)/2.
4. Reconstruct A, B, C from the cnt_k values.
Complexities: O(N) time to read the input, O(log N) extra work per test case, O(1) additional memory besides the input array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (dominated by reading the N+1 values).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) to store the f array of the current test case.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().strip().split()))
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = next(it)
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = next(it)
        f = [next(it) for _ in range(N+1)]
        S = f[0]
        A = B = C = 0
        k = 0
        while (1 << k) <= N:
            idx = 1 << k
            delta = (f[idx] - S) // idx
            cnt = (3 - delta) // 2  # 0..3
            if cnt > 0:
                A |= 1 << k
                cnt -= 1
            if cnt > 0:
                B |= 1 << k
                cnt -= 1
            if cnt > 0:
                C |= 1 << k
                cnt -= 1
            k += 1
        out_lines.append(f"{A} {B} {C}")
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
